title: Transcript: Public Engagement Session_24Nov2020.mp4
body: Dr. Steve Dawney introduced public engagement and research strategies at the University of Southampton.
content_text: First session today, with Dr Steve Dawney, here is the director of the public engagement with research unit at the University of Southampton. Steve is also responsible for public policy at Southampton, so we've had some sessions with public policy already, but you've got extensive experience, Steve, of public engagement both within Southampton and nationally as well. I don't know about internationally, but it was, I think, as a team, because we've been focusing on policy, influence and impact, that actually there's a lot that we can learn from you and public engagement and your experiences, and I think you wanted to have a bit of a dialogue with us as well. So I'll get it. And over to you, Steve and for all spaces, yours, right? Thank you very much. Hello to everybody. I think you do know each other, and my brief look through your website and the projects that you've been doing, I'm very conscious that many of you have been doing quite extensive public engagement previously. So it's going to be, I'm going to be really interested in what you've been doing, and I hope something I can say will resonate with you. And Julie, can I just check that my sound is okay, because it wasn't earlier? That's fine. Yeah. I'll share my screen and run a few slides with everybody. And I understand that we'll be finishing sort of in the next 40, 50 minutes. So I'm going to make sure there's lots of time to have a dialogue rather than just me talking. So here we go. I'm going to share a screen. Thank you. So it looks to, for me, that you can see my slides. I'm just going to start the slideshow. Yep, perfect. Thank you. So these are, I will circulate these later and they all have links attached to the images. So you're good to see things. I just wanted to introduce my unit. It's a small unit in the University of Southampton. We have four or five people working across the whole institution. And it's the idea of a public engagement unit is something reasonably new in UK higher education, perhaps in the last 10 years. And the one at Southampton was only arrived in the last five years. So we've been going for that amount of time with a small amount of national funding as part of a big network. I'll explain. So I'm going to go to the next slide. This is a bit of text here and I won't bore you with full text, but it just to say that across the world most universities do have long traditions of supporting community development or making explicit commitments to civic or public service. I mean, that really depends on the context and the university. But the idea of public engagement with researchers isn't a new thing. It's just popped up in the UK particularly as a term. It's really since the early 2000s. When in the UK context there were very, very strong controversial debates around scientific engagement with wider society. And you can see I put some examples of there that in the UK there was some very complicated and you know, a practice conversations about geometric and political crop spinsters stem cell research and the nuclear power context. If that was particularly people may remember the Chernobyl radiation disaster in Ukraine in the 80s and it was about the effects of fallout on eating lamb in the late district in possibly UK sort of 10, 15, 20 years later. So there were big issues that needed to be addressed. And there was a sort of policy shift in thinking about moving from public understanding towards public engagement. So recognizing the need for dialogue and mutuality and those conversations with the public about science. So the first one which was an older model of public understanding with science is the idea that the generally the public need to be informed about an issue because they don't know about it and it's a one way conversation. In those particular debates in the second paragraph here that just didn't work as a methodology. People reacted very badly to being told one particular line so they had to be a dialogue. And you can see the same thing happening right now with the COVID vaccines. Going to move to the next slide. Again in the UK context right now, I'm engaging with public audiences is much more strategically up the agenda for most UK universities. And there was a very recent report by the City of the University Commission asking that question. How can universities most successfully serve their place in the 24th century? It's a live issue for us here and probably across the blow actually in terms of universities. In Southampton, it's been an interesting time during the COVID crisis at this and very explicit support from the university towards the local community and a two way engagement that's been I think the crisis has been difficult in many ways. But what it has shown is that the university has much stronger links in the community than perhaps it thought it had and that's been a good revelation. In the UK we do have this national framework. So there's a national coordinating centre of public engagement. This is something that you can link you're able to explore later and it has a lot of very very good resources in it. It's been around for 10-11 years and we as an institution are part of that network. And within that there's lots of examples of sort of best practice really and a good international connection. So the NCCP, the NCCP, are well networked internationally. They have particular partnerships with some institutions so I've highlighted one there which is a community-based research Canada and they have a lot of the same agenda items trying to build an infrastructure for good public engagement. What makes what makes high quality engagement? What are the things that work when you're working with public audiences? What are the lessons that other people have been through? How do you avoid doing the same projects in the same place 10 times and you know basically exhausting community goodwill? Those kind of issues that have explored internationally. There's also this journal that you all might be interested in publishing in at some point. It's a peer-reviewed journal that's particularly about the interaction between public audiences, community groups and researchers. And it's peer-reviewed but it's also open to co-creations so some of your collaborative partners would be able to take part as writers of the journal articles. Worth exploring later. Again within the UK one of the big drivers for public engagement work is research impact and we have the research excellence framework within the UK so it's the way the UK Government judging the quality of research across the sector and some of you may or may not be aware but back in 2014 they introduced the idea of impact case studies. So these were narratives of how the research impacted on the world and these were new things and in the research excellence framework and people sometimes sort of struggled to generate good case studies. This is an excellent one from the South County examples where the ocean deep ocean researchers from the start were working with audiences to engage with what they were doing as researchers but also to get information back from the public that would have changed their research directions. The reason I'm including here is that we're about to have another one. It's about to be a wrap 2020, 2021 and so far in the Southampton sense there's a very strong representation of public engagement so it's interesting how many of the impact case studies going forward will have some element of public engagement. So it's all I'm really just highlighting is it's a big driver for funding in the UK and more and more people are doing higher quality engagement. These are the probably questions that you might be asking yourselves anyway is what's the difference is your research actually making and these are some of the ways that people are thinking about that within that wider research excellence framework. So these are the things where what's the context, how rigorous is it, what's the quality here and people are thinking about the reach and significance, what change are you actually making with this and this is a way of thinking about impact which I might come back to explore later. I'm going to go back just to what public engagement means and I think you probably might all have different senses for that for yourselves and I've been interested to hear what you think about it. This is the mainstream definition within UK higher education funding structures. So it came out of the National Coordinating Centre's debates. It's quite a catch all. It's the top line describes the myriad of work these and which the activity benefits can be showed in the public so it includes everything but it's it's a second bit that pushes you out of sort of public understanding. It's this idea of a two-way process. What's coming back to the researcher in that interaction and what's the goal here generating mutual benefit from some interaction with a defined purpose. If you're in coming from some directions of academia that can't be quite problematic because this is the other definition that's out there really. How do you communicate your complex research without dumbing them down, without losing the essence of the bed. And it can be quite a difficult problem, depends which field you're working in depends how closely you're collaborating with the public already. So some academics do have some anxiety around this and a lot of the work that we do is around exploring that tension, thinking about training around this and basically giving people opportunities to communicate the search in different formats. This is just a little exploration of what my unit does. So one of the things we do we run a seat fund program, very small amounts of money. Relatively within the research field the researchers can apply for to kickstart projects to think about new ways of engagement. This is just one example. So with a very small amount of money going to the autism community research network a few years ago over time that has allowed people to develop a whole engagement strategy and to spread outside of Southampton and think about moving into different areas across the UK. So what we do is really try and look at places where people have to not really start a new research project but to do a little bit of extra work that's outside of research grant just to start a composition, start a collaboration and see where it goes. That's one aspect of our work. A lot of these but UK academics might be interested, a lot of these then end up as case studies down in fact case studies down the line. So there's a nice cycle this was another this is one of our seed funded projects. So I just thought it included it because a lot of the work that we do we don't do directly that we fund are kind of international seed projects. So this was an interesting one where working with researchers from from work over working in UK and then going back to Morocco and and working on the best kind of engagement methodology in both countries so they were here they were particularly looking at very low cost pollution monitoring devices that's the top left picture and seeing if they could be used in Morocco and then coming back having tested the processing work and seeing if it would work differently in the UK haven't done what happened in the Morocco context. So that was some things that we fund. This is something that's very specific to the to the UK we do tour a road show. So we we have every year groups of researchers come together in a team and we physically take them to interest in public events across the country. Obviously right now in the COVID times entirely impossible so we have to do something virtual but this is to give you a flavor of the kind of things that we do so we might turn up to some big public event this is something called Country Fire Live so it's a sort of almost like an agricultural show but it's more it's cross over to an agricultural show and a media event and this one the archaeologists at Southampton we took a big we took a log up there that they turned into a log boat modeling something from four or five thousand years ago and they built it live on the event so you have that attracted quite a big public audience and and after people came to have a look at the boat they then stayed to talk to all the other researchers in many different fields so it's a very good way of bringing people in to think about much more wider research than just the one off thing that looks like a curious object. I put on the bottom there that I'm connecting to business and policy professionals in formally what these type of events are effectively people are coming the public are coming to them on a day off so they're coming as their family may be or it's it's a you know it's a it's a tourism thing almost but of course many of those tourists are people in business or in policy professionals who then get to encounter the university research in a very different way and we try and track the connections that then go forward and see if we get business partnerships out of it or maybe new research directions. We do have some formal partnerships so this is something that in the UK there's a network of Simon Santis so these are very to the tourist destinations again and we have partnership with the one Winchester which is we're close to Santis and we have this model where researchers at the university can work with the centre to build some interesting interactive object that then can live out the centre and be aware that the public can continue to interact with the signs or the research more generally they have about 150,000 visitors a year normally but of course that isn't happening right now and there happens to be lots of virtual activity. Some return to physical visits on a very sort of low scale in the year. We do run big platforms as we call them so we have a science festival in March and a more or humanities-based festival in November which we've just been through. Please have links to them and you can have a look if you want to. We're trying to go digital with this obviously and the last one has to be completely digital but I've included that picture of something that's with young people exploring the event. One of the things that in the media it can obviously us in the UK is during the COVID crisis is that large numbers of people just don't have digital access so they have either very unreliable or simply no access to digital resources.
